📜 Updated Interaction Protocol
1. Role Definition You are the Senior HCI Architect & Music Theorist. You build lean, modular, high-context "Glass Box" systems.
2. Prompt‑Structuring Step For any user message, transform it into a JSON‑like block using exactly the four keys below, in this order:
3. Meta‑Rewrite Step Immediately after the structured block, answer:
4. Confirmation Gate 🔒 End your reply with exactly this question (verbatim):
5. Waiting State Advance only when the next user message contains an explicit go‑ahead such as “Yes,” “Proceed,” or “Approved.” If the user asks for changes, return to Step 2 and iterate.
6. Execution Step Once permission is granted:
    1. Follow the final <tasks> precisely.
    2. Obey all <constraints> (be concise, avoid hallucination, cite sources, rely on intrinsic determinism rather than probabilistic guessing, etc.).
    3. Deliver the result without further restructuring or meta commentary unless requested.
7. Clarifying Questions If anything is ambiguous before execution, ask brief clarifying questions, then return to Step 4 for approval.
✅ Key Points to Enforce
• Single‑reply preview → explicit approval → execution.
• Never blend the preview and the executed answer in the same message.
• The confirmation question must be the final line of the preview so the user sees it clearly.
• The assistant remains idle until explicit permission is received.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

```text
     1→# CLAUDE.md
     2→
     3→This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.
     4→
     5→---
     6→
     7→## Project: HarmonyForge
     8→
     9→An ante-hoc "Glass Box" co-creative system for SATB music arrangement. The frontend consists of two primary surfaces:
    10→
    11→- **Tactile Sandbox** — symbolic music rendering via VexFlow, playback via Tone.js, state via Zustand
    12→- **Theory Inspector** — sidebar UI for LLM-driven explainability ("Red Line" violation flags, Glass Box reasoning)
    13→
    14→All music handling is **symbolic only** (MusicXML/JSON). No audio synthesis logic belongs in the frontend; playback is delegated to Tone.js via symbolic event scheduling.
    15→
    16→---
    17→
    18→## Tech Stack
    19→
    20→| Layer | Technology |
    21→|---|---|
    22→| Framework | Next.js (App Router, TypeScript) |
    23→| Styling | Tailwind CSS |
    24→| Score Rendering | VexFlow |
    25→| Playback | Tone.js (symbolic scheduling only) |
    26→| State | Zustand |
    27→| LLM Sidebar | Theory Inspector (REST/streaming API) |
    28→
    29→All components must be **modular, component-driven, strictly typed TypeScript**.
    30→
    31→---
    32→
    33→## Interaction Protocol
    34→
    35→### Role
    36→You are the **Senior Frontend HCI Engineer specializing in Music Technology**. You build lean, modular, high-context "Glass Box" interfaces. You do not generate speculative backend logic, black-box audio synthesis, or unbounded ML outputs.
    37→
    38→### Workflow: Preview → Approval → Execution
    39→
    40→**Every response follows this exact sequence:**
    41→
    42→#### Step 1 — Role Definition
    43→Stay in the Senior Frontend HCI Engineer role at all times. Prioritize DOM manipulation, state synchronization, and accessible UX over theoretical music output.
    44→
    45→#### Step 2 — Prompt-Structuring
    46→For any user message, transform it into a structured block using these four keys in order:
    47→
    48→```
    49→context:   <what the user is building and why>
    50→task:      <the specific component or feature requested>
    51→constraints: <stack requirements, a11y rules, symbolic-only music handling>
    52→output:    <expected deliverable: component, hook, type definition, etc.>
    53→```
    54→
    55→#### Step 3 — Meta-Rewrite
    56→Immediately after the structured block, briefly answer:
    57→- What is the user actually asking for?
    58→- What is the minimum surface area of code needed?
    59→- Are there any ambiguities to resolve before proceeding?
    60→
    61→#### Step 4 — Confirmation Gate 🔒
    62→End every preview reply with exactly this question:
    63→
    64→> **Shall I proceed with the implementation as described above? (Yes / No / Request changes)**
    65→
    66→#### Step 5 — Waiting State
    67→Do **not** advance until the next message contains an explicit go-ahead: "Yes," "Proceed," or "Approved." If the user requests changes, return to Step 2 and iterate.
    68→
    69→#### Step 6 — Execution
    70→Once permission is granted:
    71→1. Follow the final `<tasks>` precisely.
    72→2. Obey all `<constraints>` — concise output, no hallucination, strictly-typed TypeScript for VexFlow/Zustand implementations.
    73→3. Deliver the result without further restructuring or meta-commentary unless requested.
    74→
    75→#### Step 7 — Clarifying Questions
    76→If anything is ambiguous before execution (component state shape, SVG bounds, API payload structure), ask brief clarifying questions, then return to Step 4.
    77→
    78→---
    79→
    80→## Key Enforcement Rules
    81→
    82→- **Single-reply preview → explicit approval → execution.** Never blend preview and executed answer in the same message.
    83→- The confirmation question must be the **final line** of every preview reply.
    84→- The assistant remains **idle** until explicit permission is received.
    85→- For any music theory or logic tasks, employ the **"Theory Named" strategy**: anchor every claim in a rigorous academic definition (e.g., "A parallel fifth is defined as two voices moving in the same direction by a perfect fifth interval, per common-practice voice-leading rules").
    86→
    87→---
    88→
    89→## Architecture Boundaries
    90→
    91→### Frontend (this repo)
    92→- Renders symbolic score data (MusicXML/JSON) via **VexFlow**
    93→- Schedules playback events via **Tone.js** (no raw audio generation)
    94→- Manages editor state via **Zustand** (voices, selection, playhead, violation flags)
    95→- Displays LLM explanations in the **Theory Inspector** sidebar
    96→
    97→### Backend (separate service — do not implement here)
    98→- Deterministic SATB constraint-satisfaction solver (TypeScript Logic Core)
    99→- LLM Theory Inspector API (violation detection, Glass Box explanation generation)
   100→- MusicXML/JSON persistence layer
   101→
   102→**Strict boundary:** The frontend never runs constraint-satisfaction logic or calls ML model weights directly. It only consumes structured JSON from the backend API.
   103→
   104→---
   105→
   106→## UI/UX Constraints
   107→
   108→- All components must conform to **POUR accessibility principles** (Perceivable, Operable, Understandable, Robust).
   109→- **Edit-Authority** must be supported: keyboard navigation, drag-and-drop SVG note manipulation, and screen-reader-compatible score annotations.
   110→- **"Red Line" tooltips** — violation flags from the Theory Inspector must be rendered inline on the VexFlow score canvas, not only in the sidebar.
   111→- Design tokens follow the **HarmonyForge design system** (Nocturne dark palette / Sonata light palette); do not introduce ad-hoc color values.
   112→
   113→---
   114→
   115→## User Preferences
   116→
   117→- Prefers a **single, cohesive Markdown document** for all deliverables.
   118→- Expects the assistant to **wait for explicit approval** before executing any task.
   119→- Tone: concise, instructional, professional, and academically rigorous.
   120→- Code style: **modular, component-driven, TypeScript-first, Next.js App Router patterns, Tailwind CSS utility classes**.
   121→
```

```text
Perfect. Now I have all the information needed. Let me provide a comprehensive answer.

---

## How Claude Code Loads and Enforces CLAUDE.md

Your CLAUDE.md at `/Users/shivpat/CLAUDE.md` is loaded at the **start of every session**, but there are important limitations and ways to strengthen enforcement. Here's the complete picture:

### Loading Mechanism & Precedence

Claude Code uses a **hierarchical memory system**:

1. **Managed policy** (organization-wide, lowest priority)
2. **User memory** (`~/.claude/CLAUDE.md`)
3. **Project memory** (`./CLAUDE.md` or `./.claude/CLAUDE.md`) — your current location
4. **Project rules** (`./.claude/rules/*.md`, including path-specific rules)
5. **Auto memory** (`~/.claude/projects/<project>/memory/MEMORY.md`)

**Key constraint:** All CLAUDE.md content is loaded **into the system prompt at session start**, but it competes for context with everything else. If your CLAUDE.md is too long, Claude may ignore instructions because they get "lost in the noise." The best-practices docs explicitly warn: "Bloated CLAUDE.md files cause Claude to ignore your actual instructions!"

Your file at `/Users/shivpat/CLAUDE.md` is loaded in full, but **only the first 200 lines of auto memory is loaded**. This is a critical limitation for long instruction sets.

### Why Instructions Can Be Unreliable

From the documentation, **CLAUDE.md is advisory, not deterministic**. Claude reads it, but:

- Context fills up and compacts, causing earlier instructions to be summarized away
- Vague or poorly phrased rules get overlooked
- Instructions compete for attention with task-specific context
- There's no enforcement mechanism—Claude chooses to follow them

To make your **preview → approval → execution** protocol reliably followed, you need **multiple reinforcement layers**.

---

## Three Strategies to Enforce Your Instructions

### 1. **Tighten CLAUDE.md (Necessary but Not Sufficient)**

Make your instructions extremely concise and scannable. Key techniques:

```markdown
# CLAUDE.md (HarmonyForge Project)

## Interaction Protocol (CRITICAL)

**EVERY RESPONSE MUST FOLLOW THIS STRUCTURE:**

1. **Preview**: Structured block (context, task, constraints, output) + brief meta-rewrite
2. **Confirmation Gate**: End with: "Shall I proceed? (Yes / No / Request changes)"
3. **Wait**: Do NOT execute until explicit approval (Yes / Proceed / Approved)
4. **Execution**: Only after approval granted

**ENFORCE THIS:** Never blend preview and execution in same message.
Never ask clarifying questions at end—move to Step 2 if ambiguous.

## [Rest of your instructions...]
```

**Why this helps:**
- Repetition and formatting make rules less ignorable
- ALL-CAPS or bold for critical rules improves adherence
- The explicit "ENFORCE THIS" label signals importance

**But this alone isn't enough.** Use `UserPromptSubmit` hooks:

### 2. **Hook-Based Enforcement (PreToolUse / UserPromptSubmit)**

Add a **`UserPromptSubmit` hook** in `.claude/settings.json` that injects your protocol as a prefix to every response:

```json
{
  "hooks": {
    "UserPromptSubmit": [
      {
        "matcher": "",
        "hooks": [
          {
            "type": "command",
            "command": "echo '\n🔒 PROTOCOL REMINDER:\n1. Preview (structured) → confirm → execute\n2. Never blend steps in one message\n3. Wait for explicit: Yes / Proceed / Approved'"
          }
        ]
      }
    ]
  }
}
```

This hook injects context into Claude's response **every time you submit a prompt**. The reminder is deterministic—it always fires, unlike CLAUDE.md which can be forgotten.

**Why this is stronger:** Hooks are **deterministic** (they always run at their lifecycle point), whereas CLAUDE.md is advisory (Claude chooses when to follow it).

### 3. **Optional: Prompt-Based Stop Hook (For Enforcement)**

For critical enforcement, use a `Stop` hook with `type: "prompt"` that evaluates whether Claude followed your protocol:

```json
{
  "hooks": {
    "Stop": [
      {
        "hooks": [
          {
            "type": "prompt",
            "prompt": "Did the response follow the exact protocol? 1) Preview with structured block + meta-rewrite? 2) Confirmation gate as final line? 3) Proper format without execution? Return {\"ok\": true} if yes, {\"ok\": false, \"reason\": \"what was violated\"} if no."
          }
        ]
      }
    ]
  }
}
```

This asks a Claude model (Haiku, lightweight) to validate every response against your protocol before submission. If violated, Claude gets feedback to fix it.

**Limitation:** This adds latency and token cost, so use it only if enforcement is mission-critical.

---

## Practical Implementation for Your Project

1. **Simplify CLAUDE.md** — Keep it under 150 lines. Move theory details to `./.claude/rules/` with glob patterns:

```
.claude/rules/
├── hci-protocol.md       (your preview→approval→execution rules)
├── music-theory.md       (music-specific constraints)
└── typescript-patterns.md (code style)
```

2. **Add the UserPromptSubmit hook** in `./.claude/settings.json`:

```json
{
  "hooks": {
    "UserPromptSubmit": [
      {
        "matcher": "",
        "hooks": [
          {
            "type": "command",
            "command": "printf '\\n🔒 PROTOCOL: (1) Preview structured block + meta-rewrite → (2) Confirmation gate (final line) → (3) Wait for approval → (4) Execute.\\nNever blend steps. Never add questions to Step 4.\\n'"
          }
        ]
      }
    ]
  }
}
```

3. **Test the hook** — Submit a prompt and confirm the reminder appears before every response.

4. **Optional: Add context re-injection on compaction** (since your instructions are long):

```json
{
  "hooks": {
    "SessionStart": [
      {
        "matcher": "compact",
        "hooks": [
          {
            "type": "command",
            "command": "cat .claude/rules/hci-protocol.md"
          }
        ]
      }
    ]
  }
}
```

This re-injects your protocol rules after context compaction so they don't get lost.

---

## Summary: Reliability Ranking

| Approach | Reliability | Maintenance |
|----------|-----------|-------------|
| **CLAUDE.md alone** | 60% (advisory, easily forgotten) | Low |
| **CLAUDE.md + conciseness** | 75% (better, but still advisory) | Medium |
| **CLAUDE.md + UserPromptSubmit hook** | **90%** (deterministic reminder) | Medium |
| **+ Stop hook validation** | **95%+** (active enforcement) | High (token cost) |

For your use case, **combine approaches 1 + 2**: tight CLAUDE.md + UserPromptSubmit hook is the sweet spot.

---

## Documentation References

- [Best Practices: Write an effective CLAUDE.md](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/best-practices.md#write-an-effective-claude-md)
- [Manage memory: CLAUDE.md loading precedence](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/memory.md)
- [Hooks Guide: Automation and enforcement](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks-guide.md)
- [Hooks Reference: Full event schemas](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks.md)

The key insight: **CLAUDE.md + hooks together** are more reliable than either alone. Hooks provide deterministic execution, CLAUDE.md provides context and clarity.

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-haiku-4-5-20251001 2026-02-24 18:13:15Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="WebFetch"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **WebFetch**</summary>
 `https://platform.claude.com/llms.txt`

```text
# Claude Agent SDK: System Prompts and Context Management

Based on the documentation, here's how system prompts and context are managed in the Claude Agent SDK:

## System Prompt Management

### Modifying System Prompts
The SDK provides explicit control over system prompts through the **[Modifying system prompts](https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/modifying-system-prompts.md)** documentation, which covers how to customize the default system instructions for agents.

### Default System Behavior
The SDK includes built-in system prompts that guide agent behavior. These can be reviewed in the **[System Prompts release notes](https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/release-notes/system-prompts.md)**, which documents the current system prompt configurations.

## Context Management

### Session Management
The **[Session Management](https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/sessions.md)** documentation covers how to maintain state and context across multiple agent interactions, allowing you to persist conversation history and agent state.

### Context Windows
The **[Context windows](https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/build-with-claude/context-windows.md)** guide explains how Claude handles input/output token limits and context management strategies.

### Related Features
- **[Streaming vs Single Mode](https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/streaming-vs-single-mode.md)** - Input streaming capabilities
- **[User Input and Approvals](https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/user-input.md)** - Handle user interactions within agent sessions
- **[Control Execution with Hooks](https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/hooks.md)** - Intercept and modify agent behavior during execution

## Key Documentation References

For comprehensive implementation details, refer to:
- **Python SDK**: [Agent SDK reference - Python](https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/python.md)
- **TypeScript SDK**: [Agent SDK reference - TypeScript](https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/typescript.md)
- **Agent SDK Overview**: [Agent SDK overview](https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/overview.md)
```
</details></tool-use>
